% Render a figure if its PDF exists yet, else a labelled placeholder box. Lets the
% pre-registered draft compile before the run produces figures.
\newcommand{\figorbox}[2]{%
  \IfFileExists{../figures/#1}{\includegraphics[width=#2\linewidth]{#1}}{%
    \fbox{\parbox[c][0.30\linewidth][c]{#2\linewidth}{\centering\itshape

\title{The Validation Gap: When and Why Offline Metrics Fail to Predict Robot Policy Success}
\author{Eswar Krishnan}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Imitation-learned manipulation policies are deployed by choosing one checkpoint from the many
saved during training. Because measuring task success requires expensive robot rollouts,
practitioners pick this checkpoint by validation loss on held-out expert actions, a criterion
inherited from supervised learning. The proxy is known to be unreliable: the lowest-validation-loss
checkpoint can be far worse than the best one achievable. Yet the size of this discrepancy, what
causes it, and how it varies across tasks remain uncharacterized, and no offline alternative has
been evaluated systematically. We name the discrepancy the \emph{validation gap} and study it as
a surrogate-endpoint calibration problem, the way a clinical biomarker is validated against the
outcome it is meant to predict. We train Diffusion Policy and ACT across eight LIBERO and
Robomimic tasks spanning short-to-long horizons and light-to-heavy contact, and score every
checkpoint with eight rollout-free offline metrics against ground-truth closed-loop success. We
characterize the gap, measure its dependence on task regime, and ask whether any offline metric
predicts success better than validation loss and generalizes to held-out tasks. We give
particular attention to whether signals of global trajectory coherence, such as distributional
drift from the training manifold and open-loop replay fidelity, outpredict signals of local
action accuracy.
% RESULTS SENTENCE — add after the run, e.g.:
% "We find that <metric> recovers the deployment ranking that validation loss discards,
%  most strongly for <regime>, and that selecting checkpoints by it yields <X> pp higher
%  expected success than validation loss."
To our knowledge this is the first systematic calibration of offline policy-selection metrics
against deployment success in robot manipulation. The protocol is pre-registered, needs no robot
hardware, and runs on consumer GPUs.
\end{abstract}
